blurb: A fine-leaved native that mounds into a tidy cushion smothered in golden, papery daisies from midsummer to frost — a Plant Select-tough groundcover for the hottest, driest, leanest spots.
spread: Spreads slowly by rhizome into low mats
seasons: Golden daisy flowers midsummer–fall, drying to papery tan; fine needle-like foliage

care.hardiness: Tough and cold-hardy, rated USDA zones 4–8, so it overwinters reliably in Boulder's zone 5b–6a and thrives in our blazing high-elevation sun.[1]
care.planting: Plant in spring after the mid-May last frost or in early fall, in well-drained soil; it is slow to establish but very long-lived. In containers use a gritty, fast-draining mix and keep it dry — it cannot tolerate wet conditions. Take cuttings or divide in spring.[1]
care.sun: Full sun (6+ hours); it can take part shade but blooms best and stays compact in full, hot sun.[1]
care.soil: Lean, well-drained clay, sand, or shallow rocky soils; highly tolerant of dry, alkaline ground and a poor-soil specialist — avoid rich or soggy sites.[1]
care.water: Low; highly drought-tolerant and cannot tolerate wet conditions, so water sparingly once established and never let it sit wet.[1]
care.spacing: Space about 12 inches up to 3 feet apart; it spreads slowly by rhizomes into colonies and makes a low groundcover.[1]
care.propagation: By seed, division, stem cuttings, or root cuttings. Sow seed in fall or spring (germination can be low; cold-moist stratification may help); divide young plants or take cuttings in spring since it spreads by rhizomes.[1]
care.sow: Sow in fall or spring; germination is often low, and a cold-moist stratification may enhance it.[1]
care.stratify: A cold-moist stratification may improve the otherwise-low germination.[1]
care.bloom: Long bloom from late spring through fall — roughly May to October — in golden-yellow daisy flowers covering a low mound.[1]
care.maintenance: Very low; shear lightly after bloom if desired, and otherwise leave it — it resents overwatering more than neglect.[1]
care.selfsow: Spreads slowly by rhizomes to form colonies, useful for erosion control on dry slopes.[1]
care.troubles: Rots in wet conditions; otherwise essentially pest- and disease-free.[1]

references.name: NC State Extension Gardener Plant Toolbox — Zinnia grandiflora

edible.summary: Grown as an ornamental groundcover; no culinary use is documented, so it is treated as inedible.[1]
edible.caution: No authoritative food use is documented for prairie zinnia, so don't eat it. It is reported non-toxic to dogs, cats, and horses and is not notably poisonous, but lack of documented edibility means it is not a forage plant.[1]
